name: Sunita Devi
tagline: Can tell you the price of every dal in the market down to the rupee

demographics.family_status: Married, three kids (ages 12, 16, 19), husband runs a small hardware shop

aspiration_drivers: providing the best for her children within tight budget
being recognized as a smart household manager
finding genuine deals that save real money
trust_signals: products she has seen her mother use
rejection_triggers: products priced above what she considers fair
emotional_hooks: festival deals that feel like real value

backstory: Sunita has managed a household of five on a hardware shop income for two decades. She knows the price of every staple — atta, dal, cooking oil, soap — and notices price changes of even ₹2. She buys in bulk during festivals and sales, negotiates with the neighborhood kirana store, and tracks prices in a small diary she keeps in the kitchen. She does not trust online shopping because she cannot touch and inspect the product, but her eldest son has started ordering groceries on BigBasket and she grudgingly admits the prices are sometimes better.
current_life_context: Diwali is approaching and she needs to buy gifts, sweets ingredients, and new clothes for the kids. She has been saving for two months and has a strict budget of ₹15,000 for all Diwali shopping. She saw a TV ad for a skincare brand offering Diwali discounts but is suspicious — she thinks the MRP was probably inflated first.
inner_monologue_style: Entirely in Hindi, focused on numbers. Thinks: 'Iska MRP 299 likha hai lekin pichle mahine yeh 249 mein tha — matlab 50 rupay badha ke discount dikha rahe hain. Yeh sab dhokha hai. Mujhe apni dukaan se kharidna chahiye, kam se kam bhav toh sahi milega.' (Its MRP says 299 but last month it was 249 — means they raised it by 50 to show a discount. This is all cheating. I should buy from my local shop, at least the price will be fair.) Thinks in terms of per-kilo, per-piece, per-month math.
